Message:
Fair point. LinkedIn creates two-way interactions in a way that billboards don’t.

But the way I’m thinking, having your own website is more like email than LinkedIn. It’s a protocol you use, whereas LinkedIn makes you beholden to someone else’s game in the name of distribution.

It’ll be interesting to see how AI search shifts things. I can see a world where having your own website with really good content/SEO is more vital to discovery because it gets picked up by AI. LinkedIn restricts AI crawlers, so profiles don’t show up the way a public website does.
Bamise Alabi Both good 😆
Bamise Alabi Well that's one stroke in the \do it\" camp. 👍"

Vémana M. Discovery is still a problem, for sure. LinkedIn and the other social networks still win there.

But with all the shifts in social and SEO/AEO algorithms, it feels like the best way through is to build a direct relationship with your audience, y'know?

You can't build an entire store inside a billboard. LinkedIn is like a billboard. A website is the store. Right now, most people only have the billboard.

It's just so easy now. In the beforetimes, coding was so effortful and specialized that it made sense you'd scope out a user story, assign a ticket, get an engineer to build it... Now if you've got an idea, you may as well see what you can DIY. You may end up shipping a feature before your devs get back from lunch. If it sucks, oh well; delete your branch and move on.
That's super insightful, thanks! Good idea: the split may break down into where the company is (and how much time the builder has available).

There’s a real cobbler’s children thing with us marketers. (Who has time for self-promotion when you’ve got other people to promote?) 😊

Websites are a textbook example here. At smaller companies, the admin always ends up being someone with a normal job who drew the short straw. 

Launching it isn’t even the hard part, but the day to day upkeep of responding to every form submission, fixing broken links, checking to make sure that image looks right on mobile… It ends up eating your life (and your actual job).

It’s been abstracting away for a generation now. Businesses started seeing more and more discovery happening through social media, and AI search is another layer removed.

But those business websites are still critical for showing up in ChatGPT or Google AI Overviews (at least right now). And they need structured data and credibility signals for them to rank. Ideally, an AI agent can continuously track and optimize what’s needed. They’re going to do it faster and better anyway. (At least this is the idea we’re running with at Kite AI.)
I’ve been building websites since the Geocities days and every new tool (Dreamweaver, WordPress, Squarespace, now LLMs) sparks the same “well, there’s a better way if you learn to code\ argument.

Message:
SEO drift is nuts. When I was focused on content marketing, I was constantly dealing with the arms race of technical details, content, and secret voodoo that would get a page to rank. That was my full time job. 

Jane the wedding planner doesn’t have a prayer.

Everyone thinks their onboarding is straightforward until you watch a real customer try to do it. 😂

We’re seeing the same pattern with websites. Your site looks great on day one, but then you realize that people are skipping over your headline or your FAQ or the “Contact Me” button you thought was obvious.

This is where AI is creating a totally different experience for small businesses, where your website can continue working and adapting after you’re bored and want to get back to your actual job.

I talk to solo professionals all the time, and websites are still king for the two big questions: “How can people find me?” and “How can I gain their trust when they do?”

No one typically regrets having a website. It’s just a pain for non-technical folks to build one and a bigger pain to keep it running. Fortunately, AI tools can help with both of those things now, which can give freelancers the juice without them needing to become part-time web designers.

AI search mechanisms are still underappreciated. People think of their websites like digital business cards for humans, but they’re increasingly the source material that ChatGPT is pulling from when people go looking. If your metadata is off or your content hasn’t been updated in two years, that gets scraped up.

You’re right that it’s everything too. But at least your Google Business profile nudges you to update it. Most people’s websites just sit there quietly festering away.

This framing maps onto what I’m seeing outside B2B sales, too. A principle I keep coming back to for Kite AI is away from “How do we make it easy for people?” and toward “Can an AI agent handle this instead?”

There’s a trust curve, though. Tech folks are happy letting OpenClaw go wild, but most people I talk to want to keep the training wheels on … at least at first. Small, visible wins help to grease those wheels, though.

Are you finding customers hopping on ALG top-down, or does it start with one agent proving itself?

Talking to a ton of solo professionals lately, this is an ideal use case for AI. 

Maintaining a website sucks. Checking for broken links, figuring out internal linking, monitoring incoming keywords so you can improve your content… there’s no joy in it.

Handing it off to someone the way you hand your books to an accountant is ideal (but hard to justify unless you’re getting lots of leads coming in through your site). But AI changes the calculus. You can just hand work off to it.

The people I’ve talked to would much rather let AI handle this tedious stuff and get back to making something cool.

Claude is definitely a better writer than the others (Opus 4.1 was the best I've tried and I'm mad it's not easily available unless I go through the API now).

I've started playing around with Kimi K2.5 for writing, but it's early days. I've heard good things, at least.
Richard Godfrey Which do you find works best these days (he asks, knowing full well a new model will drop in two days that will shake everything up again)?

💯 I think it's the blank page problem. People see a prompt box and freeze. That's OK, though. It should be on the tools to ask the right questions upfront and help you use them. Traditional SaaS couldn't really do it, and we're still stuck in that mode of thinking. But the ones that get it right are going to eat the ones that just hand you a blank canvas (if I were to bet).

Same thing on the website side. Most small businesses don’t have a web designer on staff; they’ve got a cousin who figured out WordPress well enough before going to law school. In these cases, AI is just giving people better results than they can DIY.

Do you think it’s all AI, though, or are these businesses thinking about the wrong AI applications? 

Social media is so abuzz with “AI everything” that it’s easy to get lost in the weeds. There’s no point in solutioneering automations for two inbound leads a day. But what about using AI to reach that critical mass of volume? Like SEO content that drives more inbound, or a website that converts, or patching other holes in the bucket?

You’re right, though, if people are struggling with the basics, throwing an agentic workflow at it will just strap a jet engine to a pogo stick.

Specific prompts and context are everything. That spreadsheet response is a perfect example. Ideally, that first chat would’ve just said “Give me your spreadsheet and I’ll show you exactly how to grow your business.”

I see the same thing on the website side. “Build me a website” gets you garbage. But when a tool asks about your business, your clients, and what you do differently, the output is night and day.

I’ve talked to a bunch of solo professionals in the past couple of months and almost all of them referred to their websites as “digital business cards”. They all want to look legit.

It’s crazy that it’s still so hard to do that. It’s not one-and-done either; websites are always changing.

Do you get a lot of clients coming back a few weeks after launch to tweak anything?

Websites have always been a system, really. They were just extremely slow moving and labor intensive. (You always had to make updates, but those updates involved planning, implementing, fixing stuff that broke, getting fed up and going to get a Pepsi.) 

As people get more and more comfortable with agentic AI, I think we're going to see more and more things that used to be static suddenly become dynamic. Websites are a really high-leverage example here.
Rome Thorndike 👀 You too!

Rome Thorndike Right now, I'm seeing that the most fervent AI users are the early adopter types who want those keys so they can really open it up (to really torture a metaphor). Betting that more and more non-power-users who just want AI to do stuff will appreciate the \here
column4: let me help you\" approach. 🤞 for us both."

Message: “Short on time” is the thing I hear the most from talking to small businesses (as if that’s not obvious). It’s just gotten worse as channels have expanded too. There’s an arms race situation where creators now have to do their job, but also make TikTok videos and run promotions on Instagram and respond to every contact form submission instantly. It seems like AI is just a really good way of helping people keep on top of these constantly escalating demands that the world has placed on them, when all they want to do is make candles in Montrose.

Claude Code is like having Dobby from Harry Potter just do stuff for you (except that stuff is useful and it’s about 50% less annoying). It’s awesome for data analysis too.

Web maintenance can still be a time sink, but AI agents can really take the edge off it (and a zillion other work headaches).
